subject: ap-chemistry
title: Kinetic Molecular Theory & Gas Behavior
stimulus: A student investigates gas behavior using a rigid cylinder with a movable piston of negligible mass, as shown in the diagram above. The cylinder contains 0.325 mol of $\text{O}_2(g)$.

parts.prompt: The cylinder has a volume of 7.95 L at 25°C and 1.00 atm. Calculate the density of the $\text{O}_2(g)$, in g/L, under these conditions.
parts.scoring_notes: Mass of $\text{O}_2 = 0.325\text{ mol} \times 32.00\text{ g/mol} = 10.4$ g. Density $= m/V = 10.4\text{ g} / 7.95\text{ L} = 1.31$ g/L. Alternatively, verify using ideal gas law: $V = nRT/P = (0.325)(0.08206)(298)/(1.00) = 7.95$ L (consistent), then density $= 10.4/7.95 = 1.31$ g/L.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated density in g/L: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$m = 0.325\text{ mol} \times 32.00\text{ g/mol} = 10.4\text{ g}$
$d = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{10.4\text{ g}}{7.95\text{ L}} = 1.31\text{ g/L}$

wrong_examples: Using molar mass 16.00 g/mol (atomic O) instead of 32.00 g/mol — $\text{O}_2$ is diatomic.
Dividing moles by volume instead of grams by volume — density requires mass (g), not moles.
common_traps: Density of a gas = mass/volume. Molar mass of $\text{O}_2 = 2 \times 16.00 = 32.00$ g/mol. Convert moles to grams first, then divide by the given volume.

parts.prompt: Attempting to change the density of the $\text{O}_2(g)$, the student opens the valve on the side of the cylinder, pushes down on the piston to release some of the gas, and closes the valve again. The temperature of the gas remains constant at 25°C. Will this action change the density of the gas remaining in the cylinder? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: No, the density does not change. When some gas is released and the piston is pushed down, both the mass and the volume of gas decrease proportionally (at constant T and P, density = PM/RT, which depends only on T, P, and molar mass — all of which are constant). Equivalently: mass decreases, volume decreases by the same factor, so m/V stays the same.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer ('No, density remains the same') with a valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: No, the density remains the same.
At constant T and P, mass and volume decrease proportionally, so density (m/V) is unchanged; OR density = PM/RT depends only on P, M, and T, all of which are constant.
alternatives.correct_example: No, the density of the remaining gas does not change. When gas escapes and the piston is pushed down to maintain 1.00 atm, both the mass and volume of the remaining gas decrease by the same proportion. Since density $= m/V$, and both decrease proportionally, the ratio stays constant. Equivalently, $d = PM/(RT)$, and P, M (molar mass of O₂), and T are all unchanged.

wrong_examples: Saying density increases because less gas in the same volume — the piston moves to maintain pressure, so volume also decreases.
Saying density decreases — there is no reason for density to change at constant T and P.
common_traps: The piston is movable, so volume adjusts to maintain constant pressure. At constant T and P: $d = PM/RT$. P, T, and molar mass M are all constant → density is constant regardless of how much gas remains.

parts.prompt: The student tries to change the density of the $\text{O}_2(g)$ by cooling the cylinder to −55°C, which causes the volume of the gas to decrease. Using principles of kinetic molecular theory, explain why the volume of the $\text{O}_2(g)$ decreases when the temperature decreases to −55°C.
parts.scoring_notes: According to kinetic molecular theory, temperature is proportional to the average kinetic energy of gas molecules. Lowering temperature decreases the average speed of $\text{O}_2$ molecules. Slower molecules exert less force per collision on the piston walls. To maintain mechanical equilibrium (pressure = atmospheric), the piston descends until the frequency and force of molecular collisions per unit area are restored — which happens at a smaller volume. The result is a smaller volume at lower temperature (consistent with Charles's Law).

scoring_points.description: For a correct KMT explanation linking lower temperature to decreased molecular speed and resulting volume decrease: 1 point

required_elements: Lower temperature → lower average kinetic energy/speed of molecules.
Slower molecules exert less pressure on the walls/piston, so the piston descends (volume decreases) until pressure equilibrium is restored.
alternatives.correct_example: When the temperature decreases, the average kinetic energy (and speed) of the $\text{O}_2$ molecules decreases. The molecules collide with the piston walls with less force and frequency per unit area, so the external atmospheric pressure pushes the piston inward. The volume decreases until the pressure from molecular collisions again equals the external pressure. A smaller volume with the same number of slower molecules restores the collision rate needed to balance the external pressure.

wrong_examples: Saying the molecules get smaller — KMT relates volume change to molecular speed, not molecular size.
Citing only 'molecules slow down' without explaining how that causes volume to decrease.
common_traps: KMT: T ∝ average KE ∝ speed². Lower T → lower KE → slower molecules → less force per wall collision → piston compresses inward → volume decreases. This is the microscopic explanation for Charles's Law ($V \propto T$ at constant $n$ and $P$).

parts.prompt: The student further cools the cylinder to −180°C and observes that the measured volume of the $\text{O}_2(g)$ is substantially smaller than the volume that is calculated using the ideal gas law. Assume all equipment is functioning properly. Explain why the measured volume of the $\text{O}_2(g)$ is smaller than the calculated volume. (The boiling point of $\text{O}_2(l)$ is −183°C.)
parts.scoring_notes: At −180°C, just above the boiling point of $\text{O}_2$ (−183°C), the molecules are very close to condensing. At low temperatures, intermolecular attractive forces (London dispersion) between $\text{O}_2$ molecules become significant. These attractions pull molecules closer together, reducing the volume below the ideal prediction. The ideal gas law assumes no intermolecular attractions, so it overestimates the volume under these conditions.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing intermolecular attractive forces not accounted for by the ideal gas law: 1 point

required_elements: At very low temperatures, intermolecular attractive forces between $\text{O}_2$ molecules become significant.
These attractions reduce the actual volume below the ideal prediction.
alternatives.correct_example: At −180°C (just above the boiling point), intermolecular attractions (London dispersion forces) between $\text{O}_2$ molecules become significant. The ideal gas law assumes that gas molecules have no intermolecular attractions, but at low temperatures and high effective pressures, these attractions pull the molecules closer together. The actual volume is therefore smaller than what the ideal gas law predicts because the molecules are being drawn toward each other, effectively reducing the space they occupy.

wrong_examples: Citing molecular volume (finite size) — finite size of molecules would increase the actual volume above ideal, not decrease it.
Saying the gas partially condenses — while close to the boiling point, the explanation should focus on intermolecular forces in the gas phase making volume smaller than ideal.
common_traps: Two deviations from ideal: (1) finite molecular volume → actual volume > ideal; (2) intermolecular attractions → actual volume < ideal. At low T and near condensation, attractions dominate → actual V < ideal V.
